title: Why Adults Can Learn Russian Like Children Do
subtitle: The Science Behind the Method
author: The Russian Method
description: A science-based explanation of why traditional language learning fails and how the immersion approach replicates the way children naturally acquire language. Includes neuroscience research and practical principles.

title: Trial Lesson — Lesson 1: Love
subtitle: Your First Step Into Real Russian · $5
author: The Russian Method
description: Everything included in the trial lesson: professional audio narration, bilingual lesson text with language toggle, and a step-by-step practice assignment. One lesson, one word, the beginning of a genuine language center in your brain.

title: Your First Step Into Real Russian
subtitle: The Trial Lesson — Full Sales Page · $5
author: The Russian Method
description: The complete trial lesson sales page — everything you need to know about the method, what's included, the neuroscience behind it, and how to get started for $5.

title: Your Russian Is Not Broken. It's Frozen.
subtitle: The Advanced Track — The Russian Method
author: The Russian Method
description: For intermediate and advanced learners who already speak Russian but freeze under pressure. Explains why more conversation practice alone won't fix the plateau — and what actually will.

title: Fluency Assignment №1
subtitle: A Free Diagnostic for Intermediate & Advanced Learners
author: The Russian Method
description: A short Russian text and three spoken tasks — no dictionary, no preparation. Read aloud, retell, give your opinion. Reveals exactly where your Russian stands in under 20 minutes.

title: Distinct Cortical Areas Associated with Native and Second Languages
subtitle: Original fMRI Research — Nature, 1997
author: Kim, Relkin, Lee & Hirsch
description: The original Nature paper demonstrating via fMRI that Broca's area contains spatially distinct regions for native and second languages acquired in adulthood.

title: When an Adult Adds a Language
subtitle: It's One Brain, Two Systems
author: Sandra Blakeslee
description: A groundbreaking article explaining how second languages learned in adulthood occupy a separate region of the brain compared to languages acquired in infancy.
